# Write policy plugins

Plugins provide a way to extend Terraform policy with additional functions that you can use to customize your policy authoring experience. Write Terraform policy plugins in Golang using the [`terraform-policy-plugin-framework`](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-policy-plugin-framework.git) open source library.

@include "beta.mdx"

You must compile Terraform policy plugins and include them in your policy VCS repository before you can use them in your policies. In your policy files, use the `core::<functionname>` syntax to refer to functions built into Terraform or Terraform policy. Use the `plugin::<pluginname>::<functionname>` syntax to refer to functions from plugins that you write.

## Author a plugin

To author your own plugins, create a new Go package, import the Terraform policy plugin framework library, and then author a `main()` function to register your plugin's functions and Go functions to implement each of them.

The following is an example of a plugin that defines two functions, `echo`, and `trim`:

<CodeBlockConfig filename="plugins/src/example/main.go">

```go
package main

import (
  "strings"
  "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-policy-plugin-framework/policy-plugin/plugins"
)

func main() {
  plugins.RegisterFunction("echo", echo)
  plugins.RegisterFunction("trim", trim)
  plugins.Serve()
}

func echo(value string) (string, error) {
  return value, nil
}

func trim(value, prefix string) (string, error) {
  return strings.TrimPrefix(value, prefix), nil
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

After you implement your plugin in Go, build it into a binary with the `go build` command, and store both the source and binary in the same VCS repository as your policy files.

## Plugin directory structure

Set up the following directory structure for your plugin to ensure Terraform policy can read it:

```text
./
├── policies/
├── tests/
├── plugins/
│   ├── src/
│   │   └── example/
│   │       ├── main.go
│   │       ├── go.mod
│   │       └── go.sum
│   └── bin/
│       └── example
└── README.md
```

When you enforce your policies in HCP Terraform, your VCS repository must also contain the plugin binary.

## Compile a plugin

Before you can use your plugin in your policies, you must compile it.

Change into the plugin source directory.

```shell-session
$ cd plugins/src/example
```

Initialize go modules.

```shell-session
$ go mod init example
```

Download and verify go modules.

```shell-session
$ go mod tidy
```

Build the plugin.

```shell-session
$ go build -o ../../bin/example main.go
```

## Use plugins in policies

Specify plugins by using the `plugins` block within the `policy` block of your `.policy.hcl` files. Refer to [`policy`](/terraform/policy/reference/policy/policy) for more information.

<CodeBlockConfig filename="policies/example.policy.hcl">

```hcl
policy {
  plugins {
    example = {
      source = "../plugins/bin/example"
    }
    second_example = {
      source = "path/to/second-bin"
    }
  }
}

locals {
  instance_type = plugin::example::trim("foo.t3.micro", "foo.")
}
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

The value of the `source` attribute must be a string that is the relative path to a binary in the same VCS repository as your policies. The binary must be a valid Terraform policy Go plugin server. Use the [`terraform-policy-plugin-framework`](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-policy-plugin-framework) open source library to ensure your plugin provides the correct interface.

In your policies, reference your plugin functions with the `plugin::<plugin_label>::<function_name>(<arguments...>)`.

## Requirements

You must precompile function plugins in order to execute policies that include on HCP Terraform. Terraform policy assumes that a precompiled binary is available with the appropriate directory structure when you run your tests or configure the policies into a policy set in HCP Terraform.
